Mission director Dr. Oluwaseun Adebayo checks the telemetry feed every morning from her office in Abuja; the signal takes 4 hours to reach her and arrives as a whisper.

events.title: Ocean Ecosystem State
events.description: The world's oceans are now the largest managed ecosystem on Earth: 10,000 automated aquaculture platforms produce 200 million tons of protein annually, while AI-guided 'reef printers' deposit calcium carbonate structures that mimic natural coral. Norwegian marine engineer Dr. Sigrid Haugen calls it 'a garden, not a wilderness -- beautiful in its way, but we should mourn what it replaced.'

events.title: AI Maturity
events.description: The Global AI Authority, headquartered in Zurich under Director-General Dr. Fatou Diallo of Senegal, must approve every AI system exceeding 10^18 parameters before deployment. The approval process takes 18 months on average; tech companies call it 'the Zurich bottleneck.' But there have been zero AI safety incidents under the regime, and Diallo's stock answer to critics is three words: 'You're welcome. Next?'

events.title: Arctic Settled
events.description: The Arctic Council 2.0, expanded to include China, India, and the African Union, manages shipping lanes carrying 30% of global trade, a network of research stations, and 14 marine protected areas. Commissioner Aleksei Volkov signs 400 permits per day from his office in Murmansk, where roses now bloom in the garden outside -- a fact he finds 'beautiful and obscene in equal measure.'

events.title: Climate Equilibrium
events.description: Twelve stratospheric aerosol injection platforms and a constellation of 200 orbital sun-shields maintain global temperatures at 1.8C -- but the system can never be turned off. The 'Climate Maintenance Authority,' funded by a mandatory 1.5% global GDP levy, employs 500,000 people worldwide. Director Dr. Yuki Sakamoto calls it 'an air conditioning bill for a planet that will never be cool again on its own.'

events.title: Global Governance 2075
events.description: The 'Stability Pact,' led by China and backed by 60 nations, maintains global order through a shared surveillance network that monitors every digital communication on Earth. Dissent is flagged, scored, and suppressed before it can organize. Human Rights Watch was declared a terrorist organization in 2073. The system delivers peace, prosperity, and zero privacy; exiled journalist Mikhail Sorokin broadcasts from a pirate satellite: 'The cage is comfortable. The food is good. It is still a cage.'

events.title: Global Population 2075
events.description: A combination of universal life extension access, pro-natalist incentives, and robotic eldercare has stabilized population at 7.9 billion. The 'Singapore Compromise' -- generous parenting support paired with longevity treatments that keep grandparents healthy enough to help raise children -- has become the global template. Birth rates haven't risen; people have simply stopped dying on schedule.

events.title: Solar System Development
events.description: The asteroid mining conglomerate Orbital Resources Inc. posted $800 billion in revenue in 2074, making CEO Hiroshi Tanaka-Williams the wealthiest human in history. Lunar tourism serves 50,000 visitors annually at $200,000 per ticket, and the zero-gravity manufacturing platform 'Forge-7' produces semiconductor crystals of a purity impossible on Earth. Space is not a frontier -- it's a factory floor with a view.

events.title: US Status 2075
events.description: The US commands the world's second-largest economy, third-largest military, and first-largest cultural export industry. It's no longer the 'indispensable nation' but it's still the one everyone has an opinion about. Ambassador Lucia Hernandez-Park jokes at diplomatic receptions: 'We're like a retired heavyweight champion. Nobody wants to fight us, but everyone still watches us at parties.'

events.title: Last Great War
events.description: Three warheads detonated: one over Taipei, one over Vladivostok, one intercepted 12 kilometers above the Sea of Japan. Taipei ceased to exist in 4.7 seconds; 2.8 million people died in the fireball. The 'Black Week' that followed saw the global economy contract 40% and 14 governments fall. Survivor Lin Wei-Chen, who was 8 years old and shielded by a concrete overpass, became the face of the aftermath; her radiation burns are visible in every photograph taken of her since.
events.narration: The final collapse of the authoritarian surveillance order came not through gradual reform but in the nuclear fires of January 2078, when the Sino-Russian Compact's orbital defense grid malfunctioned during a routine enforcement action against the European Federation. Within eighteen hours, Mumbai, Marseille, and Vladivostok lay in ruins, their combined death toll reaching 4.2 million before the emergency ceasefire protocols activated through the surviving AI governance networks. The war's brevity—historians would later call it the "Forty-Hour War"—masked its decisive impact on the century's trajectory, as the delicate balance between digital and physical civilizations that had emerged a decade earlier shattered completely. By February, the old world order of managed climate systems and regulated AI development had dissolved into competing regional blocs, each guarding their technological assets with unprecedented secrecy.

events.title: Climate Recovery
events.description: At 2.4C, Earth is a different planet than the one humanity evolved on, but civilization has bent rather than broken. Floating cities house 50 million in Southeast Asia; the agricultural belt has shifted 800 kilometers north; and air conditioning consumes 18% of global energy. Children read about 'old weather' in school the way previous generations read about the Ice Age -- as something real but personally unimaginable.

events.title: Demographics 2085
events.description: Bulgaria, Latvia, and South Korea have each lost more than half their population since 2025. China has fallen below 800 million. Rural Japan has been entirely depopulated; the last village school in Akita prefecture closed in 2079 and was converted into a museum of childhood. Meanwhile, Central Africa's population continues growing, creating a grotesque imbalance: a continent of youth surrounded by a world of empty cradles.

events.title: Economic Model 2085
events.description: Universal Basic Income of $2,400/month (in 2085 dollars) is paid to every human on Earth, funded by a 4% global wealth tax and AI productivity dividends. Markets still function, billionaires still exist (though the richest human 'only' controls $400 billion), and the startup culture thrives in Lagos, Bangalore, and Buenos Aires. Swedish economist Dr. Erik Lindgren calls it 'capitalism with a conscience -- 60 years late, but better than never.'

events.title: Existential Risk Assessment
events.description: Nuclear war is less likely, but AI misalignment risk is higher. Bioweapons are better defended against, but nanotechnology weapons exist that weren't imagined in 2025. Climate is stabilizing, but the psychological toll of chronic crisis has produced a global epidemic of nihilism. Risk theorist Dr. Amir Hosseini of Iran describes it as 'running on a treadmill of existential threats: we're working harder than ever and staying in exactly the same place.'

events.title: Human Identity
events.description: A family dinner in 2085 might include a biological grandmother, an enhanced mother with neural mesh, a father who exists as a digital consciousness projected into a synthetic body, and a teenage daughter who is genetically edited for radiation resistance because she plans to emigrate to Mars. They argue about the same things families have always argued about: who does the dishes, whether the music is too loud, and what it means to live a good life.

events.title: Interstellar Prospects
events.description: Sixty years of research and $500 billion have produced a definitive answer: the stars are unreachable. No propulsion system can overcome the energy requirements; no biological or digital human can survive the radiation environment; and no economy can justify the cost. Astrophysicist Dr. Maria Santos-Nakamura, who spent her career trying to prove otherwise, gave her retirement lecture at MIT with characteristic bluntness: 'The universe is not designed for tourism.'

events.title: Middle East 2085
events.description: The 'New Andalusia' -- as cultural historians call the region's renaissance -- has produced the century's most celebrated literary movement, three Nobel laureates in a decade, and the world's most-visited museum (the Mosul Institute of Human Heritage, built on the ruins of what ISIS destroyed 70 years earlier). Iraqi architect Zaha Hadid III designed the building as 'a wound that became a window.'

events.title: World System 2085
events.description: Three separate internets. Four incompatible currencies. Two distinct AI safety regimes. The blocs have turned inward so completely that a student in Nairobi cannot access a library in Shanghai, and a researcher in Boston needs a six-month visa to attend a conference in Beijing. Historian Dr. Amira Khalil of Cairo calls it 'the new Middle Ages: walled kingdoms, suspicious of strangers, hoarding knowledge behind high barriers.'

events.title: AI-Human Relationship
events.description: The 'Separation Doctrine,' written into the constitutions of 140 nations, maintains a hard boundary: AI manages infrastructure, logistics, and data; humans make all decisions involving ethics, art, relationships, and governance. The boundary is enforced by law and reinforced by culture. A child in 2095 knows instinctively what is 'AI work' and what is 'human work' the way a child in 2000 knew the difference between indoor and outdoor voices.

events.title: China 2095
events.description: The 'Seven Chinas' -- Guangdong, Shanghai, Sichuan, Beijing-Hebei, Yunnan, Manchuria, and Tibet-Xinjiang -- each operate as independent states with separate governments, currencies, and foreign policies. The split was formalized in 2088 after the last Premier-General resigned. Cantonese and Mandarin are drifting into mutual unintelligibility. Historian Dr. Li Wei of the Shanghai Republic wrote: 'China was always an empire pretending to be a nation. The pretense finally ended.'

events.title: Civilization Verdict
events.description: Population: declining. Biodiversity: declining. Democratic governance: declining. Trust between nations: declining. The only things rising are temperatures, inequality, and the dosage of antidepressants prescribed globally. Historian Dr. Marcus Jefferson III of Howard University delivered his assessment in a voice so quiet the microphones barely caught it: 'Civilizations do not usually know they are falling. They feel it as a kind of tiredness. We are very, very tired.'

events.title: Europe 2095
events.description: The 'Copenhagen Standard' -- universal healthcare, AI-assisted education, 24-hour work weeks, and guaranteed creative sabbaticals -- has been adopted by 60 nations. Europeans live an average of 114 years, work less than any society in history, and produce more art per capita than any civilization ever has. The criticism, delivered by American economist Dr. James Chen, is also telling: 'Europe solved every problem except boredom.'

events.title: Final Climate State
events.description: The Climate Maintenance Authority celebrates its 30th anniversary in 2095. Its stratospheric aerosol fleet, orbital mirror array, and ocean alkalinity program hold temperatures at +1.8C -- but the system runs on a $5 trillion annual budget and can never be turned off. Dr. Sow described it as 'the most expensive life-support machine in history, keeping a planet alive that we nearly killed. The bill comes due every year, forever. Our grandchildren will pay it, and their grandchildren after them.'

events.title: Global Governance 2095
events.description: Forty-seven entities claim sovereign authority over some part of the human population: nation-states, city-states, corporate polities, digital nations, and two AI systems that govern their own territories without human consultation. No one is in charge. No treaty binds everyone. A Somali fisherman, a Singaporean augmented trader, and a digital consciousness in Luminara live under three entirely different legal systems and share a planet they cannot agree on how to manage.

events.title: Global Population 2095
events.description: Six point eight billion humans, down from a peak of 9.7 billion three decades ago. Every country on Earth is shrinking except Nigeria, which stabilized at 400 million. The world has learned to contract gracefully: cities consolidate, infrastructure is right-sized, and AI fills the labor gaps. The most poignant statistic: there are more people alive over 80 than under 10. Pediatrician Dr. Aisha Nwosu of Lagos now sees more robots in her waiting room than children.

events.title: Space Civilization 2095
events.description: Asteroid mining alone generates $8 trillion annually; orbital solar power beams energy to 2 billion people; and the zero-gravity pharmaceutical industry has produced cures for three cancers that proved impossible to synthesize on Earth. Space is not a frontier -- it's an industrial district. The 30,000 workers on the Ceres mining platform live in company towns, eat company food, and joke that they traded one form of gravity for another: 'Up here, the corporation is the only thing holding you down.'

events.title: United States 2095
events.description: The 'American Confederation' is six nations wearing a trenchcoat: Cascadia (progressive, tech-driven, population 45 million), the Gulf Federation (energy-rich, conservative, population 60 million), New England (academic, aging, population 30 million), the Great Lakes Republic (industrial, pragmatic, population 50 million), the Mountain West (libertarian, sparse, population 15 million), and the Southern Compact (culturally distinct, population 70 million). They share a military, a space program, and a football league. Everything else is separate.

events.title: Century Verdict
events.description: Population: down 30%. Biodiversity: down 50%. Democratic nations: down 40%. Trust in institutions: at a century low. The generation born in 2095 is the first in modern history expected to live shorter, poorer, and less free than its parents. Midnight in Nairobi was quiet. The fireworks were cancelled due to air quality. Dr. Osei looked into the camera and said: 'The century asked if we could do better. We could not. The next century will ask the same question. I hope those who answer it are braver than we were.'
